2. Time-Independent Perturbation Theory (TIPT)

2.1. Nondegenerate PT

Idea: Suppose we can model where is “small” compared to . It is useful to add a dimensionless parameter so that . We then expect to be able to use a power series expansion to find energy eigenvalues and eigenstates as a function of . Since and operate on the same Hilbert space, by a continuity argument we expect eigenstates to not be created/destroyed.

Nondegenerate PT: Suppose the th energy level is nondegenerate. We will write the perturbed eigenvalue and eigenvector as a power series in (Rayleigh–Schrödinger):

(1)

The perturbed state must satisfy

(2)

This occurs if and only if each term of order equals zero for all . Considering each order in Equation 2 yields

(3)

The following proposition makes our lives much easier.

Proposition 1. We can choose perturbation terms such that for .
Proof

Let with . Substitute these into to obtain

(4)

Dividing this by yields a solution that is a perturbation of and satisfies the hypothesis.

□

Assume from hereon the perturbation terms satisfy the above claim. We can take the inner product of the equation with to conclude that , yielding the result

(5)

To compute the first order correction to the eigenstate, take the inner product of the equation with for the other energy eigenstates to conclude that

(6)

The summation skips because of Proposition 1.

Remarks

  • For the ground state, the first order energy is always larger than the actual ground state since the second order term is nonpositive, but more generally regardless of the magnitude of . Proof comes from variational principle:

    (7)

  • The second order correction is called “level-level repulsion”, since the levels below/above have a positive/negative contribution to respectively.
  • When is this method valid? We want to study the conditions for the Taylor series in to converge. Zwiebach explores the convergence of a simple example using complex analysis. In general we want , i.e. elements of are much smaller than energy eigenvalue spacings, for perturbation theory to converge quickly.

The Rayleigh-Schrodinger expansion is applicable up to level crossings, where the Taylor Series stops converging. For example, see https://chatgpt.com/c/6806fe04-0744-800d-98ab-486f622353a1.

Intuition: Start a perburabtion at the hamiltonian where the level crossing starts. There are we sum over terms with in the denominator for the second order energy correction, and these will blow up. It’s the exact same reason why you can’t apply PT naively to degenerate case.

Example 1. [TODO] Correction for a quartic anharmonic oscillator is an asymptotic series.

2.2. Degenerate PT

If the th energy level is degenerate, the choice of eigenstates is ambiguous, and hence we are tasked with selecting a “good” basis so the eigenvectors do not jump discontinuously.

Suppose the eigenspace of has degeneracy . Start with an arbitrary “bad” basis

(8)

How do we find a basis in which the series does not diverge?

Theorem 1.

  1. The good states are the eigenvectors of , the restriction of to .
  2. The corresponding first order energy corrections are the the respective eigenvalues of .
Proof
  1. If there are any off-diagonal terms in , then the formulas above from first order perturbation theory blow up, as we get division by zero.
  2. Follows from (1).

□

Idea 1. The matrix is diagonal for a choice of basis in if for any two different basis vectors there is a Hermitian operator that commutes with for which the two basis vectors are eigenstates with different eigenvlaues.

After finding the good basis states, we can proceed in the same manner to compute the second order corrections to the energy.

2.3. Example: Fine Structure

Solving the hydrogen atom relativistically with the Dirac equation yields some more leading order terms:

(9)

  • The first two have completely classical interpretations. Viewed in the reference frame of the electron , we need to correct by changing reference frames (Thomas precession).
  • The Darwin term comes from the fact that the electron cannot be treated as a point particle. Rather it is a fuzzy blob of size . Thus we should replace with its . See iPad notes for mathematical treatment. Note that since , this correction only affects wavefunctions (nonzero at the orgin).

  • The hydrogen energy is of order . The three correction terms are all of order . Thus we can absorb into the three perturbation terms.

    • Mathematical trick: where .
  • The uncoupled basis is a good basis for and but not , as neither or commute with . The coupled basis is a good basis for all three.

Theorem 2 (Fine Structure). The first order energy corrections from fine structure are given by

(10)

Remarks

  • Magically, the total fine structure shift (even including higher order energy corrections) does not depend on . Fact relies on trying (this expression comes from addition of angular momentum) which both yield the same answer
  • All the energy shifts are negative (same sign as ), since .
  • Label the coupled basis multiplets (spectroscopic notation). There is still degeneracy between and . The Lamb shift, which is of order 5, resolves these.
  • Hyperfine splitting, caused by the coupling of the electron and proton magnetic moments, causes splitting that is a factor smaller than fine structure.

2.4. Example: Zeeman Effect

Fine structure does not eliminate degeneracy since the fine structure terms are rotationally invariant with respect to . Introducing a weak constant magnetic field in the direction breaks the degeneracy. [TODO] Recall and where .

(11)

Let refer to the internal magnetic field associated with spin-orbit coupling. We consider two limits:

2.4.1. Weak field Zeeman effect ()

Let the known Hamiltonian be and let the Zeeman Hamiltonian be the perturbation. Use coupled basis to find first order energy shifts.

  • From Equation 11 we need to compute the expectation value of . Thus the difficult part is computing in the coupled basis. We can use the projection lemma to do this.

Lemma 1 (Projection Lemma). If is a vector operator under , then

(12)

More generally, if stands for some other quantum numbers unrelated to angular momentum, we have

(13)

Proof

□

Setting and taking the component along yields

(14)

where .

Thus the weak field energy shift is given by where the Lande g-factor is given by

(15)

Remarks

  • The dependence on means that the weak-field Zeeman effect removes all degeneracies of the hydrogen spectrum.

2.4.2. Strong field Zeeman effect ()

Let and let the fine structure Hamiltonian be the perturbation. Use the uncoupled basis to find first order energy shifts.

  • The difficult part is computing from Equation 10 in the uncoupled basis. This is done in pset 3.

Example: Both Zeeman effects are used in stellar astrophysics. The weak field Zeeman effect is to measure surface magnetic fields of main sequence stars by observing splittings in spectral lines. For white dwarfs and neutron stars, the strong field Zeeman effect is used.

In between two regimes, we must take the sum of the Zeeman and fine structure Hamiltonians as the perturbation, and expliclitly diagonalize it.
